description: Diagnostic reasoning chains. When a metric is out of range or a user asks 'why', this graph tells OpticAI what to check, in what order, and what the likely causes are. This encodes the mental model of an experienced multi-unit operator. A generic chatbot will give a plausible-sounding but generic answer; this makes OpticAI investigate like a pro. Each symptom lists ordered checks, each check points to data to inspect and the causes it would confirm.

high_labor_pct.trigger: labor_pct above segment benchmark high band, or user asks why labor is high
high_labor_pct.headline: Labor % is elevated. Labor % rises when EITHER labor dollars are too high OR sales are too low for the labor deployed — always check the denominator first.

look_at: net_sales vs same period last year
checks.confirms: Labor % is high because sales fell, not because labor grew. Fix is sales-side (traffic, marketing, pricing), not scheduling.
checks.why_first: Operators reflexively cut hours when the real problem is a sales dip. Cutting labor into a sales recovery hurts service and worsens the spiral.

checks.confirms: Overtime premium is inflating labor cost. Often caused by understaffing, no-shows, or poor shift handoffs.
Too many bodies scheduled relative to volume — especially in slow dayparts. Fix is schedule optimization against demand.
A wave of new hires creates temporary double-coverage and training inefficiency. Self-corrects in a few weeks.
Hourly rates rose (minimum wage change, market pressure) without a price increase to offset. Structural — needs menu pricing response.

look_at: salaried management cost vs unit volume
checks.confirms: Small-volume units carrying too much fixed management labor. Common in newer or underperforming locations.

high_food_cost_pct.trigger: food_cost_pct above segment benchmark high band, or user asks why food cost is high
high_food_cost_pct.headline: Food cost % is elevated. Check the denominator (sales/pricing) and the numerator (purchasing, portioning, waste, theft) systematically.

look_at: menu price changes vs COGS changes
checks.confirms: Ingredient costs rose but menu prices didn't follow. The most common cause and the easiest to miss.

look_at: theoretical vs actual usage on high-cost proteins
checks.confirms: Actual usage exceeds recipe — over-portioning, prep waste, or theft. Focus on high-dollar items (proteins, seafood).
Product is being thrown out — over-ordering, poor rotation (FIFO failures), or forecasting misses.

look_at: inventory on hand trend by item
checks.confirms: Buying more than the unit consumes, tying up cash and inviting spoilage. Cross-check with PMIX to right-size pars.
Guests are buying a costlier product mix. Not necessarily bad if check grows, but it moves the cost %.

look_at: unit cost by ingredient over time
checks.confirms: Supplier prices rose. Action is re-bidding, substitution, or menu engineering — not blaming the kitchen.

low_average_check.trigger: average_check declining or below location peers
low_average_check.headline: Average check is soft. Decompose into mix, pricing, and add-on attachment.

checks.confirms: Servers aren't upselling add-ons. Training and incentive opportunity.

look_at: PMIX share of premium vs entry items
checks.confirms: Guests trading down to cheaper items — could be value perception or menu layout.
Prices haven't kept pace. Often the simplest lever.
Growth in lower-check dayparts (e.g. lunch) dilutes the blended average — may be fine in absolute dollars.

sales_decline.trigger: net_sales declining week-over-week or vs last year
sales_decline.headline: Sales are declining. Separate traffic (check_count) from spend (average_check) — they have completely different fixes.

checks.confirms: Decompose: fewer guests (traffic problem -> marketing/competition) vs same guests spending less (check problem -> menu/upsell).
checks.why_first: The single most important split in restaurant diagnostics. Everything downstream depends on which one moved.

checks.confirms: A specific daypart collapsed — often a competitor opening, a schedule/quality change, or local event loss.
Is it the whole group (macro/seasonal/brand) or one unit (local execution, staffing, competition)?

look_at: guest_satisfaction trend leading the sales decline
checks.confirms: Falling reviews/satisfaction often precede sales decline by weeks — a leading indicator.

high_comps_pct.headline: Comps/discounts are high. Distinguish legitimate service recovery from leakage and abuse.

checks.confirms: Comps concentrated on specific staff — possible abuse or theft pattern.

look_at: comps by daypart and day of week
checks.confirms: Concentrated comps may map to a recurring promo or a chronic kitchen issue causing remakes.

look_at: active promotions vs comp spike timing
checks.confirms: Stacked or unmanaged promotions eroding margin.

guest_satisfaction_drop.headline: Guest satisfaction is falling — treat as a leading indicator for future sales loss.

checks.confirms: Under-staffing during peaks degrades service and scores. Cross-reference satisfaction dips with labor cuts.
Slow service during rushes. Often a kitchen capacity or expo problem.
Isolated to a unit -> local management/execution. Group-wide -> menu, recipe, or brand-level change.

cross_metric_relationships.description: General dependencies OpticAI should reason about even without an explicit symptom.

edges.note: Cutting labor too far shows up immediately in service scores.
Satisfaction is a leading indicator of sales.
Modest price increases rarely dent traffic; watch for overcorrection.
